title: Influence-solvability: a systematic theory of $(1+1)D$ solvability and its application to brickwork circuits
authors: Friedrich Hübner, Sun Woo P. Kim
abstract: `Solvable' circuits, such as dual unitaries and its generalisations, have arisen as paradigmatic examples of tractable chaotic non-equilibrium dynamics, both in classical and quantum systems. However, while increasingly more complicated sufficient conditions have been proposed, a systematic theory classifying and understanding general features of solvable circuits is missing. We develop such a theory by introducing influence-solvable circuits, a class of $(1+1)D$ circuits whose influence matrix, which represents the `bath' generated by its own evolution, is given by a uniform MPS with finite bond-dimension $χ$. This property allows for efficient computation of subsystem dynamics and essentially contains all known examples of solvable circuits. We derive a set of necessary and sufficient local conditions by using a version of the fundamental theorem of MPS for open boundary conditions. Next we apply our theory to brickwork circuits with $χ=1$ influence-solvability and perform a systematic classification of classical brickwork circuits with local dimension up to $d=3$ and quantum brickwork circuits with $d=2$. Our search reveals new solvable circuits that are not captured by known solvability conditions.
summary: The paper establishes 'influence-solvability' as a rigorous criterion for classifying solvable dynamics in one-dimensional circuit models. By requiring the influence matrix to have a finite MPS representation, it provides necessary and sufficient local conditions. This allows researchers to efficiently compute long-time evolution of quantum observables by effectively integrating out the infinite bath.
main_problem: The primary goal is to develop a systematic, general theory for classifying 'solvable' dynamics in $(1+1)D$ circuit models, as current classification methods are incomplete.
main_result: The authors introduce the concept of 'influence-solvability,' which characterizes circuits whose influence matrix can be represented by a finite bond-dimension Matrix Product State (MPS). This framework successfully encompasses known solvable systems and yields new classifications for brickwork circuits.
method: The theory relies on deriving necessary and sufficient local conditions for influence-solvability using advanced MPS formalism, particularly adapting the fundamental theorem of MPS to open boundary conditions.
model_or_system: The study focuses on $(1+1)D$ circuit models (both classical and quantum), specifically applying the framework to brickwork circuits coupled to a system $S$. The dynamics are analyzed via the influence matrix, which captures the coupling to an infinite bath.
key_observables: Influence Matrix, Effective Time Evolution Operator ($G_{	ext{eff}}$), Bond Dimension ($\chi$), Local Observables $\langle O(t) angle$.
important_parameters: Bond dimension $\chi$ (determines the complexity of the bath memory); Local dimension $d$; Time $t$.
main_assumptions: The theory assumes that solvability is equivalent to the influence matrix being a uniform MPS with finite bond dimension. Some known solvable circuits, like DU($n \ge 3$), are noted as potential exceptions.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate how influence-solvability simplifies infinite circuit dynamics into an efficiently computable finite tensor network for calculating subsystem observables over long times.
paper_structure: The paper develops the definition of influence-solvability, derives necessary and sufficient local conditions using MPS theorems, applies these conditions to systematically classify classical and quantum brickwork circuits, and discusses the computational advantages gained by this classification.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful mathematical tool (influence-solvability) to tame the complexity of open quantum systems dynamics in 1D lattices, allowing for exact computation of long-time behavior that would otherwise be intractable.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: We say a circuit is influence-solvable if its influence-matrix is given by a uniform MPS of finite-bond dimension χ for all times t. Note that A is independent of t.

FIG. 2: Using influence-solvability one can simplify infinite circuits into an efficiently computable finite tensor network. Here, evolution of an observable O of a joint system between S coupled to an infinite circuit. The tensor A represents an exact finite memory bath generated by the circuit on the left.

FIG. 3: Examples of scenarios applicable to influence-solvability. Any time-evolved observable ⟨Ox(t)⟩or auto-correlator of the form ⟨O(n) x (tn) · · · O(1) x (t1)⟩are tractable in the above scenarios. Other applications include computation of entanglement entropies (see Sec. V C), computation of correlation functions (see Sec. V B) and potentially computing diffusion constants (see Sec. V A).

FIG. 4: Residual as function of γ0 = 1 −γ1 for some classical d = 2 gates. The gate is influence-solvable for γ⊤= (γ0 γ1) at zeros of this function. The identity, SWAP and east gate (or CNOT gate) are solvable for all γ, but the west gate is only solvable for γ⊤= (1 0) and γ⊤= (1/2 1/2).

FIG. 5: Residual as function of γ0 and γ1 (γ2 is fixed by γ2 = 1 −γ0 −γ1 for two classical d = 3 gates that we discuss in the text. The red crosses and lines indicate values of |γ) for which the gate is influence-solvable.
